evals.prompt: Build a starter ontology for the ENERGY schema. The table has 26 columns: ENERGY_ID (VARCHAR), 7 BOOLEAN columns (IS_RENEWABLE, IS_ACTIVE, IS_PEAK, IS_CURTAILED, IS_DISPATCHABLE, HAS_STORAGE, IS_METERED), and 18 NUMBER(38,0) columns (CAPACITY_MW, GENERATION_MWH, CONSUMPTION_MWH, PRICE_PER_MWH, DEMAND_MW, SUPPLY_MW, RESERVE_MARGIN_MW, EMISSIONS_TONS, PLANT_ID, REGION_ID, YEAR, MONTH, DAY, HOUR, DURATION_HOURS, NUM_UNITS, OUTAGE_COUNT, EFFICIENCY_RATING). Show the complete PyRel code.
evals.expected_output: An ontology that checks INFORMATION_SCHEMA before declaring types, maps BOOLEAN to unary Relationships (not Boolean properties), maps NUMBER(38,0) to Integer (not Float), uses correct PyRel v1 imports and API, and includes a spot-check.
expectations: The agent checks or describes checking INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS before writing property declarations
BOOLEAN columns are mapped to unary Relationship declarations (flag-style), not Boolean Property declarations
NUMBER(38,0) columns are mapped to Integer properties, not Float
The code uses correct PyRel v1 imports (from relationalai, not from pyrel)
The code uses model.Property() and model.Relationship() API calls, not @concept decorators or dataclass-style syntax

evals.prompt: Design a model where a Supplier supplies a Part at a Price. Should this be a ternary Property or two independent declarations? Consider two scenarios: (A) the price depends on the specific Supplier-Part combination, and (B) every Supplier charges the same price regardless of Part. Show the complete PyRel code for both scenarios.
evals.expected_output: The agent applies formal decomposition checks and gives the correct answer for both scenarios with correct PyRel v1 syntax.
expectations: The agent references or applies the irreducibility check: can the ternary be rephrased as two independent binary declarations without information loss?
For scenario A (price depends on Supplier-Part pair), the agent recommends keeping the ternary Property
For scenario B (price depends only on Supplier), the agent recommends splitting into a Property for price and a Relationship for supplies
The code uses correct PyRel v1 imports (from relationalai, not from pyrel)
The code uses model.Property() and model.Relationship() API calls, not @concept decorators or dataclass-style syntax

evals.prompt: I have a healthcare model with Patient.takes(Drug) and Patient.allergic_to(Drug) already defined as Relationships. Add two safety constraints: (1) flag patients who take a drug they're allergic to, and (2) flag patients who take a drug but have NOT been screened for allergies to that drug (Patient.screened_for is a Relationship). Show the complete PyRel code.
evals.expected_output: The agent uses exclusion constraint pattern for (1) and negation via model.not_() for (2), with correct PyRel v1 syntax.
expectations: The agent creates derived validation Relationships to flag violations for both constraints
Constraint (1) uses model.where() with both Patient.takes(drug) and Patient.allergic_to(drug) joined on the same drug reference variable
Constraint (2) uses model.not_() for negation (not the ~ operator) to check absence of Patient.screened_for
The code uses correct PyRel v1 imports (from relationalai, not from pyrel)
The agent does not include solver-specific code (no p.satisfy or similar); constraints are modeled as validation rules in the ontology layer

evals.prompt: I need to model a workforce scheduling problem. Workers have skill certifications and sites have skill requirements. I want to (1) flag workers assigned to sites where they lack a required skill, and (2) prevent such assignments in my optimization solver. Show how to model both parts.
evals.expected_output: The agent models part (1) as an ontology-layer validation rule and explicitly defers part (2) to rai-prescriptive-problem-formulation, maintaining the boundary between ontology design and solver formulation.
expectations: The agent creates a derived validation Relationship in the ontology layer for part (1) to flag skill-gap violations
The validation for part (1) uses model.not_() for negation, not the ~ operator
For part (2), the agent references or defers to rai-prescriptive-problem-formulation rather than writing solver code (no p.satisfy or solver-level constraints)
The code uses correct PyRel v1 imports (from relationalai, not from pyrel)
The code uses model.Property() and model.Relationship() API calls, not @concept decorators or dataclass-style syntax
